# Cinema Slime — Syndication Reference

> You are reading this because you want to reproduce Cinema Slime content on
> your own site. This file is self-contained: it teaches a reader with **zero
> prior knowledge** of Cinema Slime, RSS, or Nostr to read both content sources
> end to end, from nothing to content-in-hand. You should not need any other
> document, and you should not need to read a Nostr NIP. Everything required to
> execute is below.
>
> Canonical home of this file: https://cinemaslime.com/llms.txt

## §0 — Orientation

Cinema Slime publishes **two** kinds of content, each from one **stable, public,
ever-changing source**. The mental model is the same for both: a fixed address
whose contents change over time. You re-read the address to get the current set.

1. **Episodes** — audio installments of the Cinema Slime Podcast. Source: a
   single podcast RSS feed (§1).
2. **Official Essays** — long-form written pieces the brand has endorsed.
   Source: Nostr, indexed by one curation event published by the brand (§2–§4).

The Essay path has one layer of indirection: a curation event lists *pointers*
(coordinates) to Essays; you resolve each pointer to fetch the actual body.
§2 is a transport primer for readers new to Nostr; §3 is the index; §4 is the
resolution step.

What you do with the content once you hold it (render it, style it, attribute
it) is out of scope. Syndication carries no attribution or permission
obligation — the brand treats these sources as public and syndication as pure
additional reach.

---

## §1 — Episodes (podcast RSS)

**Endpoint:** `https://anchor.fm/s/1050fb0e4/podcast/rss`

It is a single, self-contained **podcast RSS 2.0** document hosted by Anchor.
Every title, description, audio file, image, and duration is inline, and every
media URL resolves to Anchor's own CDN. One fetch returns the **complete
published archive** — no pagination, no truncation (63 episodes back to the
first, at time of writing). The feed is already public and already sends
`Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *`, so you can fetch it cross-origin directly; it
has no dependency on Cinema Slime's own servers.

**About the `itunes:` tags.** The feed declares the standard podcast namespace
`xmlns:itunes="http://www.itunes.com/dtds/podcast-1.0.dtd"`. Tags prefixed
`itunes:` belong to *this* document under that namespace — they are **not** a
request to Apple, iTunes, or any other service. Read them as ordinary inline
fields.

**Per-item fields.** Each Episode is an `<item>` element. The canonical fields:

| Field | XML | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Title | `<title>` | text |
| Publication date | `<pubDate>` | RFC-822 date string |
| Description | `<description>` | HTML body of the Episode |
| Audio | `<enclosure>` | attribute `url` is the audio file. `type` (MIME) and `length` (bytes) are also present on the element. |
| Artwork | `<itunes:image>` | the `href` attribute is the image URL |
| Duration | `<itunes:duration>` | text |
| Episode number | `<itunes:episode>` | text, when applicable |
| Season | `<itunes:season>` | text, when applicable |
| Type | `<itunes:episodeType>` | `full` \| `bonus` \| `trailer`; **default `full`** when absent |
| Link | `<link>` | text |
| GUID | `<guid>` | the stable Episode Identifier (see below) |

**Identity & page URL.** `<guid>` is the stable Episode Identifier — it refers
to exactly one Episode across time and reloads. The canonical Cinema Slime page
for an Episode is:

```
https://cinemaslime.com/#/episode/<url-encoded guid>
```

URL-encode the guid (it is percent-encoded into the hash route).

---

## §2 — Nostr transport primer

The Essay index and bodies live on **Nostr**, a relay-based protocol. If you
already speak Nostr, skip to §3. Otherwise, here is everything you need.

**Relays are WebSockets.** You connect to a relay over `wss://`, send a
subscription request, and receive matching events back as JSON frames.

- Subscribe: send `["REQ", "<subId>", <filter>]` where `<subId>` is any string
  you choose and `<filter>` is a JSON object (see below).
- Receive: the relay streams `["EVENT", "<subId>", <event>]` frames, one per
  matching event, then `["EOSE", "<subId>"]` ("End Of Stored Events") once it
  has sent everything it has stored.

**Event shape.** Every event is:

```json
{ "id": "...", "pubkey": "...", "created_at": 1700000000,
  "kind": 30001, "tags": [["a","..."],["p","..."]], "content": "...", "sig": "..." }
```

`kind` is an integer that classifies the event. `tags` is an array of arrays.
`content` is a string. `created_at` is unix seconds.

**Query the union of these public relays** (an event may live on some and not
others — merge the results, then de-duplicate by `id`):

```
wss://relay.damus.io
wss://nos.lol
wss://relay.primal.net
wss://relay.nostr.band
```

**The replaceable/addressable rule (kinds 30000–39999).** For these kinds, an
event's *identity* is the triple `(kind, pubkey, d-tag)` — the value of the
`["d", "<identifier>"]` tag. A newer event with the same triple **replaces** the
older one. So a single query may return several versions of the same logical
object: **keep only the one with the highest `created_at`.** This is exactly why
the index holds pointers, not bodies — an Essay (or the index itself) is edited
in place at a stable coordinate, and you always re-read the latest version.

**Signatures (optional but recommended).** Relays can serve forged events. Each
event's `sig` is a schnorr signature over the serialized event, verifiable
against `pubkey` (secp256k1). Verifying lets you reject forgeries; it is not
required to read content.

---

## §3 — The curation event (kind:30001) — the "RSS feed" of Essays

One event is the authoritative index of Official Essays. Fetch the latest
version with this filter:

```json
{ "kinds": [30001],
  "authors": ["3fe7d91eb4133567db1ad7abab7ae308ebd9ae2d109601a7257e995035651365"],
  "#d": ["cinema-slime-essays"] }
```

The `authors` value is the brand's single hardcoded trust anchor — the one
pubkey that defines what is "official." Apply the replaceable rule from §2:
**keep the returned event with the highest `created_at`.**

This is a kind:30001 (NIP-51) curation list. Its `content` is empty — **all the
data is in `tags`:**

- **`["a", "<coordinate>", "", "<slug>"]`** — one per curated Essay.
  - Index `[1]` = the Essay **coordinate** (a pointer; resolved in §4).
  - Index `[3]` = an **optional slug** (a human-readable address; see rule below).
- **`["p", "<pubkey>", "", "<display name>"]`** — index `[3]` is the
  **authoritative author display name** the brand assigns. Use this name, **not**
  the author's own Nostr profile (`kind:0`). Match a `p` tag to an `a` coordinate
  by shared pubkey (the middle field of the coordinate).

**Coordinate format:** `kind:pubkey:identifier`

- `kind` — an integer (Essays are kind `30023`; see §4).
- `pubkey` — 64 lowercase hex chars.
- `identifier` — **everything after the second colon.** The identifier may
  itself contain colons, so split on the *first two* colons only, not all of
  them.

**Slug rule:** when present, a slug matches `^[a-z0-9]+(?:-[a-z0-9]+)*$`
(lowercase alphanumerics and single hyphens) and is unique within the list. It
is optional — an Essay can be official without one. Treat any index-`[3]` value
that fails this pattern as "no slug."

---

## §4 — Resolving a coordinate to Essay content (kind:30023)

Each `a` coordinate from §3 points to a kind:30023 (NIP-23) long-form event —
the Essay body. First parse the coordinate into `(kind, pubkey, identifier)`,
then fetch the latest version:

```json
{ "kinds": [30023], "authors": ["<pubkey>"], "#d": ["<identifier>"] }
```

Apply the replaceable rule from §2: of the events returned, **keep the highest
`created_at`** (and confirm its `(kind, pubkey, d-tag)` matches the coordinate).

**Fields** (tags unless noted):

| Essay field | Source | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Title | tag `title` | |
| Summary | tag `summary` | |
| Image | tag `image` | |
| Published date | tag `published_at` | unix seconds; **fall back to the event's `created_at`** when absent |
| Body | event `content` | Markdown |
| Identifier | event `id` | the event id |
| Author name | **§3's `p` tag**, not here | the brand-assigned display name |

**Page URL.** The canonical Cinema Slime page for an Essay prefers the slug when
the curation list gave one, otherwise the full coordinate:

```
https://cinemaslime.com/#/essay/<url-encoded slug-or-coordinate>
```

URL-encode the token (a coordinate contains colons, which percent-encode into
the hash route).

You now hold the content. Rendering it is up to you.
